cure.ref: USDA FSIS Jerky + meatsandsausages Cure Calculator
cure.note: Cure #1 (6.25% sodium nitrite) optional but recommended: 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. USDA-cited research shows cured jerky has greater bacterial destruction than uncured. Cure #1 is correct type (short-cure/heated, not long-aged) — Cure #2 would be wrong here. Current 'optional Cure #1' text is accurate.

smoke.ref: USDA FSIS Jerky / NDSU jerky extension
smoke.note: Low warm-smoke/dry chamber ~60-70°C; drying dehydrator maintains 60-70°C. This is a dry-down, not a hot cook.

safe.ref: USDA Ask-USDA: drying meat/poultry jerky temperature
safe.note: Beef jerky must reach 71°C / 160°F (pre- or post-drying heat step) since dehydrator alone will not pasteurize before drying makes bacteria heat-resistant.

age.note: Dry at 60-70°C to water activity aw < 0.75 for shelf stability; typical ~50% weight loss.

rationale: Cure #1 optional/recommended is correct as written; documented the USDA 71°C beef-jerky heat floor and aw<0.75 target.

cure.note: Poultry is higher-risk (salmonella). USDA-cited research: adding Cure #1 (2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite) markedly increases bacterial destruction. Current text names no cure — STRONGLY recommend adding optional Cure #1. Cure #1 (not #2) is correct.

safe.ref: USDA Ask-USDA: poultry jerky must reach 165°F
safe.note: Poultry jerky must reach 74°C / 165°F (higher than the 71°C beef floor) via a heat step before/after drying.

age.note: Dry to aw < 0.75; heat to 74°C before final dry-down.

rationale: FLAGGED: no cure listed for a poultry product — recommend optional Cure #1, and safety floor must be 74°C/165°F for poultry, not 70°C.

cure.note: Traditional biltong uses NO nitrite. Salt + vinegar (acid) lower pH and displace water; coriander/pepper for flavor. Current 'vinegar+salt+coriander+pepper' is correct and authentic — nitrite is not required for classic biltong.

smoke.note: No smoke — biltong is air-dried only.

safe.note: Dry-cure: not cooked. Safety from salt + vinegar (low pH) + air-drying to reduced aw — no thermal pasteurization floor.

age.note: Hang in cool moving air; ~2-4 days to firm; target ~35-50% weight loss for shelf-stable result.

rationale: Correct as-is: vinegar+salt cure with no nitrite and no smoke is authentic biltong; documented weight-loss target.

cure.note: Dry cure with Prague Powder / Cure #1 (6.25% sodium nitrite) + sugar, ~7 days. 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Cure #1 is correct (cooked/hot-smoked bacon, not long-aged). Current text is correct.

smoke.note: Meathead smokes ~107°C (225°F) to internal 65.6°C, ~2 h; current 90°C warm-smoke for 3-4 h reaches the same internal target, acceptable.

safe.note: Smoke to internal 65°C / 150°F — cooks and pasteurizes, safe to eat off the smoker.

age.note: Dry cure ~7 days at 4°C (fridge), flipping daily; rinse before smoking.

rationale: Cure #1 + sugar dry cure is correct; documented AmazingRibs 65°C internal safety target.

cure.note: Cure #1 dry cure ~7 days, 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Same method as pork bacon; Cure #1 correct (cooked/smoked, not aged). Current text correct.

age.note: Dry cure ~7 days at 4°C; rinse before smoking.

rationale: Cure #1 correct; documented 65°C internal safety target.

cure.note: Cure #1 in the ground meat: 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Correct — kielbasa is smoked and fully cooked (short process), so Cure #1 not Cure #2. Current text correct.

smoke.note: Warm-smoke rising to bring internal to ~68-71°C (154-160°F); may take hours.

safe.note: Ground/emulsified meat cooked-sausage floor 71°C / 160°F (many Polish recipes pull at 154°F/68°C; conservative displayed floor 71°C).

age.note: Cure meat 48-72 h before stuffing; not aged/dried — cooked and eaten or refrigerated.

rationale: Cure #1 in ground meat is correct for a cooked smoked kielbasa; documented cook-to floor.

cure.ref: meatsandsausages (Marianski) — curing for sausages / Cure Calculator
cure.note: Cajun seasoning + Cure #1: 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Correct — andouille is heavily smoked then cooked, a short-process product → Cure #1 not #2. Current text correct.

smoke.ref: The Black Peppercorn — Smoked Andouille (stepped smoke)
smoke.note: Traditional stepped smoke ~4 h rising 54→88°C (130→190°F), or finish in 74°C water bath, to internal ~71°C.

rationale: Cajun seasoning + Cure #1 correct for a cooked smoked sausage; documented cook-to floor.

cure.note: VERIFIED Cure #1 at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite (Marianski). Correct — this cervelat is a fermented SEMI-dry sausage that is COOKED (internal 60°C) and only briefly dried, so Cure #1 (not Cure #2) is right. Current text correct.

smoke.note: After 24 h ferment at 38°C, warm smoke starts 43°C/12 h then rises until internal 60°C (140°F). smt~60 chamber is consistent.

safe.note: Semi-dry fermented: safety from fermentation (pH drop) + Cure #1 + cook to internal 60°C, not a 71°C thermal floor. Documented, no single cook-to floor.

age.note: Ferment 24 h at 38°C, 85-90% RH; optional dry 2 days at 16-22°C, 65-75% RH to desired weight loss; store 10-15°C, 75% RH.

rationale: Confirmed Cure #1 is correct per Marianski (fermented semi-dry, cooked to 60°C); safety is ferment+cure+cook, documented in note.

cure.note: Fermentation culture + Cure #1 at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Correct — summer sausage is a fermented SEMI-dry sausage that is cooked to ~68-71°C (short process), so Cure #1 is standard, not Cure #2. Current text correct.

smoke.note: Ferment then warm-smoke rising to internal ~68-71°C (154-160°F).

safe.note: Cooked to internal 71°C / 160°F (ground meat floor); also ferment pH drop + Cure #1.

age.note: Ferment ~24 h warm/high-RH; brief dry/equalize. Semi-dry, not long dry-aged.

rationale: Culture + Cure #1 correct for a cooked fermented semi-dry summer sausage; documented 71°C cook floor.

cure.note: FLAG: current says 'fermentation culture + Cure #2' — WRONG per Marianski. Authoritative kabanosy is NOT fermented: cure with Cure #1 (2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite), hot-smoke then bake to internal 68-71°C (154-160°F), then OPTIONALLY semi-dry. Cooked product → Cure #1, not Cure #2. Change required. (Only a raw, uncooked, purely air-dried variant would need Cure #2 — not this cooked recipe.)

smoke.note: Hot smoke 40-50°C ~50-60 min then bake 60-90°C until internal 68-71°C (total ~70-90 min). smt 50 chamber consistent with the smoke stage.

safe.note: Cook to internal 68-71°C (154-160°F). Conservative ground-meat floor 71°C.

age.note: Optional semi-dry 5-7 days at 12-15°C, 75-80% RH to 45% weight loss (55% yield).

cure_text_suggestion: Cure #1 בבשר טחון (מעושן + מבושל לפנים 71°C, אופצ' ייבוש קל)
rationale: FLAGGED: Marianski's authoritative kabanosy uses Cure #1 and is cooked to 68-71°C, not a Cure #2 fermented dry sausage — corrected the cure type and removed the fermentation claim.

cure.note: Starter culture + salt + Cure #2 (6.25% nitrite + 4% nitrate) at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite plus a nitrate reservoir. Correct — long dry-cured/fermented salami REQUIRES Cure #2 (nitrate reservoir over weeks of drying). Current text correct.

smoke.note: Cold smoke optional at <20-22°C (part of drying cycle, not a cook). No hot smoke — must stay cold so it dries, not cooks.

safe.note: Dry-cure: pH/aw controlled — not cooked. Safety from ferment to pH ≤ 5.3 + Cure #2 + drying to reduced aw; no thermal floor.

age.note: Ferment 20°C, 85-90% RH ~72 h to pH≤5.3; dry 12-16°C, 80-85% RH for 2-3 months to 30-35% weight loss (shelf stable).

rationale: Culture + salt + Cure #2 correct for a long dry-cured fermented salami; documented pH/aw safety and 30-35% weight loss.

cure.note: Smoked pimentón + garlic + Cure #2 (nitrite+nitrate) at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Correct — dry-cured Spanish chorizo air-dries 6-8 weeks, needs Cure #2 nitrate reservoir. Current text correct.

smoke.note: Smokiness usually from pimentón; any smoke must be COLD (<22°C) so it dries rather than cooks.

safe.note: Dry-cure: pH/aw controlled — not cooked. Cure #2 + drying to reduced aw.

age.note: Ferment/hold 22-24°C, 85-90% RH ~48 h; dry 6-8 weeks at 12-16°C, RH stepping 85→75%; target ~30% weight loss.

rationale: Smoked paprika + garlic + Cure #2 correct for dry-cured chorizo; documented dry-cure safety and 6-8 week dry.

cure.note: Starter culture + Cure #2 (nitrite+nitrate) at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Correct — landjäger is a fermented, pressed, air-dried semi-dry sausage, needs Cure #2 for the drying window. Current text correct.

smoke.note: Marianski cold-smokes at <20°C for a few hours (to inhibit mold), then dries. If a warm-smoke ~50°C variant is intended keep short; a cold-smoke option is the traditional route.

safe.note: Dry-cure/semi-dry: pH/aw controlled. Ferment pH drop + Cure #2 + drying to ~30% loss; no thermal cook floor.

age.note: Ferment 20°C, 90-95% RH ~72 h; pressed flat; dry ~3 weeks at 12-14°C, 80-85% RH to 30% weight loss; store 10-12°C.

rationale: Culture + Cure #2 correct for a fermented dried landjäger; noted Marianski uses cold smoke <20°C, documented 30% weight loss.

cure.note: Paprika + chili + starter culture + Cure #2 (nitrite+nitrate) at 2.5 g/kg = 156 ppm nitrite. Correct — slow-fermented dry pepperoni air-dries 6-8 weeks, needs Cure #2 nitrate reservoir. Current text correct.

smoke.note: Optional cold smoke ~8 h at <22°C, treated as part of the drying/fermentation cycle, not flavoring/cooking.

safe.note: Dry-cure: pH/aw controlled — not cooked. Ferment pH drop + Cure #2 + drying to reduced aw.

age.note: Ferment 20°C, 85-90% RH ~72 h; dry 12-16°C, 80-85% RH ~6-8 weeks to ~30% weight loss (shelf stable).

rationale: Paprika/chili + culture + Cure #2 correct for a slow-fermented dry pepperoni; documented dry-cure safety and 30% weight loss.
